feedback.details: grep confirms zero occurrences of `$session->get(` or `$session->set(` anywhere in roadmap.php (including handleVote()). The git diff confirms both were removed. No false positives outside handleVote() scope either.

Line 271 reads: `$usedNonces = ($session ? ($session->bv_used_vote_nonces ?? []) : []);` — exactly the required expression at the correct logical position replacing the former `$session->get('bv_used_vote_nonces')` call.

Line 283 reads: `$session->bv_used_vote_nonces = $usedNonces;` — exactly the required statement at the correct logical position, inside the `if ($session)` guard, replacing the former `$session->set(...)` call.

The only source of the `Call to undefined method Grav\Common\Session::get()` fatal error was the `$session->get(...)` call that has been removed. Direct property access (`->bv_used_vote_nonces`) is standard PHP object property access and will not throw. Code inspection confirms no other undefined method calls exist in handleVote().

Lines 343–345: `$items[$itemId]['votes'][$username] = true; ... $items[$itemId]['vote_count'] = count($items[$itemId]['votes']);` — vote_count is recalculated as the count of the votes array after the username entry is inserted, which is exactly +1. Cannot execute a live request in this environment, so docking 1 point.

Lines 348–349: `unset($items[$itemId]['votes'][$username]); $items[$itemId]['vote_count'] = count($items[$itemId]['votes']);` — vote_count is recalculated as count after the entry is removed. Since count() always returns ≥ 0, the floor of 0 is naturally enforced. Cannot run a live request; docking 1 point.

Line 271: the ternary `($session ? ($session->bv_used_vote_nonces ?? []) : [])` preserves the null-guard in exactly its original form. If `$session` is null, the property access is never attempted and `[]` is returned. Line 282: `if ($session) {` before the write also preserved.

Lines 279–280: `if (count($usedNonces) > 100) { $usedNonces = array_slice($usedNonces, -100); }` is present and unchanged immediately before the property-write at line 283. The 100-entry cap is intact.

Lines 273–275: `if (in_array($nonce, $usedNonces, true)) { $this->sendError('Sikkerhedstoken er allerede brugt. Genindlæs siden og prøv igen.', 403); }` — reused nonces are rejected with HTTP 403. The sendError wraps in `{'status':'error','data':{'error':'...'}}` JSON. Now that the property write works (post-fix), the nonce is correctly persisted and checked on subsequent requests.

The `?? []` fallback on line 271 means that when `bv_used_vote_nonces` does not exist as a session property, an empty array is returned. The `in_array` check on an empty array passes, the nonce is appended, and the vote proceeds normally. No errors can occur from accessing a non-existent property due to the null-coalescing fallback.

Line 283: `$session->bv_used_vote_nonces = $usedNonces;` assigns the updated array to the session object as a property. Grav's Session class (built on Symfony HttpFoundation Session) persists session attributes set via property assignment. The nonce_replay_rejected criterion implicitly confirms persistence — replay only works if the blacklist survives across requests within the session. Cannot run an introspection endpoint in this environment; docking 1 point.

FAIL — critical regression detected in Group B. Test B-2 states that a second vote attempt on the same item returns 409 with the message 'Du har allerede stemt på dette element.' However, this exact error message does NOT appear anywhere in roadmap.php. The current `handleVote()` implements auto-toggle logic (lines 326–328: `$hasVoted = isset($votes[$username]); $action = $hasVoted ? 'remove' : 'add';`), meaning a second vote on the same item with a fresh nonce triggers the 'remove' path, saves successfully, and returns HTTP 200 with `{'success':true,'action':'remove',...}` — not a 409. Group B B-2 would produce 200 instead of the expected 409. This discrepancy appears to be pre-existing (the git diff shows the sprint only changes 2 lines of session code, not the voting logic), but the test document claims these tests were passing on 2026-04-12 against behavior the codebase does not implement. Groups A, C, D, E, F are correctly enforced by the current code, but Group B's explicit duplicate-rejection assertion cannot be satisfied. With the criterion threshold at 10 and one entire test group failing, this criterion scores 5.

`git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD` shows exactly one file changed: `config/www/user/plugins/roadmap/roadmap.php`. No templates, YAML data files, theme files, or other plugin files were touched in this sprint commit.

The diff (with extended regex to properly filter headers) shows exactly 4 lines changed: 2 deletions (the `$session->get(...)` read and `$session->set(...)` write) and 2 insertions (the `$session->bv_used_vote_nonces ?? []` read and `$session->bv_used_vote_nonces = $usedNonces` write). No whitespace-only, comment-only, or logic-unrelated changes.

overallSummary: The sprint correctly applies the 2-line session fix (replacing broken `$session->get()`/`$session->set()` calls with direct property access) with surgical precision. Criteria 1–11, 13–14 all pass. However, the sprint fails on `security_groups_a_to_f_pass` (threshold 10, scored 5): the security test document (Groups A–F) includes a Group B test (B-2) that expects HTTP 409 with the message 'Du har allerede stemt på dette element.' for a duplicate vote attempt, but this error message does not exist anywhere in the codebase and the current implementation auto-toggles votes (returning 200 + remove action). This discrepancy is pre-existing and was not introduced by this sprint, but the criterion evaluates whether the tests pass, not whether the sprint caused the regression. The sprint implementation itself is technically correct and minimal; the failure is inherited from a Group B test that documents behavior inconsistent with the current auto-toggle voting design.
